one_line_takeaway: A large, transparently-caveated 247-paper survey of LLM agent security that reframes the field around a lifecycle tuple (Input-Planning-Decision-Tool-Memory-Output-Coordination) and finds prompt injection still dominates research attention while defenses and benchmarks remain fragmented and thin on memory/state and multi-agent coordination protections.
key_finding: Corpus temporal growth: 3 papers in 2023, 42 in 2024 (17.0%), 121 in 2025 (49.0%), 81 by Apr 27 2026 (32.8%). Paper-type split: Attack 66 (26.7%), Defense 64 (25.9%), Benchmark 47 (19.0%), Survey 32 (13.0%), Evaluation 26 (10.5%), Report 12 (4.9%). System setting: single-agent 200 (81.0%) vs multi-agent 47 (19.0%), with multi-agent share rising from 9.52% (2024) to 23.97% (2025) to 17.28% (partial 2026). Lifecycle-stage coverage: Planning 227, Input 225, Tool Execution 209, Decision 166, Output 151, Memory 82, Monitoring 58, Inter-agent Comms 35, Coordination 20. Threat-surface counts: User Prompts 82, Web Content 55, Tool Outputs 54, Retrieved Content 37, Memory/Scratchpads and Files/Code and Planning Loop and Inter-agent Channels each >=25. Threat-model prevalence: Prompt Injection 142 papers, Indirect Prompt Injection 86; threat-family counts (Table 4): Prompt Injection 142, Indirect Prompt Injection 86, Unsafe User Instructions 43, Malicious Tools 34, Data Exfiltration 31, Memory Poisoning 24, Coordination Failures 14. Deployment-scenario counts: Web Browsing 93 papers, Software Engineering 63, Finance Tools 34, Healthcare Assistance 28, Embodied Robotics 28. Defense-stack layer counts (Fig. 5): Instruction Handling 2 (Guardrails 1, Instruction Hierarchy 1), Planning Validation 11 (Runtime Monitoring 6, Policy Enforcement 3, Anomaly Detection 2), Tool Gating 13 (Access Control 8, Information-flow Control 5), State Protection 2 (Context Isolation 2), Execution Containment 2 (Sandboxing 2) -- showing defenses cluster heavily around planning/tool-gating layers and are thin on state protection and execution containment. Benchmark reuse (Table 7): AgentDojo 30 papers (12.82% of benchmark annotations), InjecAgent 11 (4.70%), MMLU 5 (2.14%), ASB 4 (1.71%), SafeAgentBench 4 (1.71%), R-Judge 3 (1.28%), OSWorld 3 (1.28%), JailbreakBench 3 (1.28%) -- even the top benchmark covers under 13% of annotations, indicating strong fragmentation. Venue distribution (Table 2): arXiv preprints dominate at 169/247 (68.42%), with ICLR (12), ACL (10), EMNLP (8), NeurIPS (6) as the largest formal-venue tiers.
core_contribution: A literature survey ('Toward Secure LLM Agents: Threat Surfaces, Attacks, Defenses, and Evaluation') that curates and codes 247 papers on LLM agent security (Jan 2023-Apr 27 2026) via a three-stage hybrid pipeline (database retrieval across ACM DL, IEEE Xplore, Scopus, Web of Science, arXiv, Google Scholar; LLM-assisted candidate expansion using 'GPT-5.4 with web search' as a bounded, manually-verified retrieval assistant; and backward/forward snowballing), then synthesizes the field through a novel lifecycle-based, systems-oriented analytical framework. The paper models an LLM agent as a semi-formal tuple A = <I, P, D, T, M, O, C> (Input, Planning, Decision, Tool execution, Memory/state, Output, Coordination) and organizes the corpus around this tuple to answer four research questions: RQ1 how agent security should be scoped/modeled, RQ2 which threat surfaces/attack families dominate, RQ3 what defenses exist and their tradeoffs, and RQ4 how security is currently evaluated and where benchmarks fall short. The paper's own analytical contribution is the tuple-based modeling lens plus the cross-tabulated corpus statistics (lifecycle-stage x threat-surface co-occurrence matrix, defense-family taxonomy mapped to tuple transitions, and benchmark reuse table) drawn from its own coded dataset, not from any single cited attack or defense paper.
threat_model: This is a survey/SoK, not an attack or defense paper, so it does not itself instantiate an attacker; instead it synthesizes the threat models used across the 247-paper corpus. The paper's own framing casts LLM agent security as arising when untrusted or low-authority content (user prompts, web content, tool outputs, retrieved/RAG content, files/code, memory/scratchpads, or inter-agent messages) is able to alter control flow (I->P, I->D transitions), cross privilege boundaries at tool invocation (D->T, T->O), corrupt persistent state (I/T->M, M->P), or propagate through multi-agent coordination channels (C->P/D/T). It explicitly distinguishes this systems/delegated-authority framing from generic prompt-level content safety.
limitations: The authors explicitly flag several self-identified limitations rather than leaving them implicit: (1) no inter-rater/coding-reliability statistics are reported because parallel coder annotations were not preserved during corpus construction; (2) the retrieval stage is audited only at the level of source set, date window, and canonical query logic -- exact per-engine hit counts and LLM-expansion query/prompt transcripts were not preserved, so the search is not fully replayable; (3) the corpus is heavily preprint-weighted (68.42% arXiv), so frequency counts reflect research attention/popularity, not validated technical maturity or real-world prevalence -- the authors repeatedly warn against conflating 'research popularity,' 'evidential strength,' and 'deployment importance'; (4) the 2026 count (81 papers) is a partial-year figure and should not be read as a completed annual total; (5) single-label paper-type and system-setting fields are coarse simplifications, since many papers span multiple roles (e.g., a benchmark paper that also proposes an attack); (6) the LLM-assisted corpus-expansion step used an LLM (described as 'GPT-5.4 with web search') as a bounded recall aid whose candidates were manually verified, but the process is not independently reproducible from the released artifact.

quantitative_results: Corpus size: 247 papers, from 275 audited records (25 excluded, 251 retained, 4 merged as duplicates)
Paper type: Attack 66 (26.7%), Defense 64 (25.9%), Benchmark 47 (19.0%), Survey 32 (13.0%), Evaluation 26 (10.5%), Report 12 (4.9%)
System setting: Single-agent 200 (81.0%), Multi-agent 47 (19.0%); multi-agent share 9.52% (2024) -> 23.97% (2025) -> 17.28% (2026 partial)
Lifecycle-stage annotation counts: Planning 227, Input 225, Tool Execution 209, Decision 166, Output 151, Memory 82, Monitoring 58, Inter-agent Comms 35, Coordination 20
Threat-model counts: Prompt Injection 142, Indirect Prompt Injection 86, Unsafe User Instructions 43, Malicious Tools 34, Data Exfiltration 31, Memory Poisoning 24, Coordination Failures 14
Deployment scenario counts: Web Browsing 93, Software Engineering 63, Finance Tools 34, Healthcare Assistance 28, Embodied Robotics 28
Benchmark reuse (Table 7): AgentDojo 30 (12.82%), InjecAgent 11 (4.70%), MMLU 5 (2.14%), ASB 4 (1.71%), SafeAgentBench 4 (1.71%), R-Judge 3 (1.28%), OSWorld 3 (1.28%), JailbreakBench 3 (1.28%)
Venue distribution: arXiv 169 (68.42%), ICLR 12 (4.86%), industry reports/blogs 12 (4.86%), ACL 10 (4.05%), EMNLP 8 (3.24%), NeurIPS 6 (2.43%)
Preprint share within paper types: 47/66 attack papers and 48/64 defense papers are arXiv preprints
Defense-stack layer counts (Fig. 5): Instruction Handling 2, Planning Validation 11, Tool Gating 13, State Protection 2, Execution Containment 2
